summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – where the husband failed to attend the final hearing – where the third and fourth respondents did not participate in the proceedings – where the final hearing proceeded on an undefended basis – where the husband failed to make proper disclosure – where the Court considered the evidence of family violence perpetrated by the husband against the wife – where the husband spent a period of time in prison for the breach of a domestic violence order – where the husband unilaterally transferred joint property without accounting for the same –where it is just and equitable to make an order – where the Court considered contributions, future circumstances and justice and equity in accordance with the normal principles. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – where the wife made an instanter application seeking an order in relation to passports for two children – application granted.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HOWARD J

These reasons for judgment were delivered ex tempore on 9 March 2026 and have been settled and corrected for grammatical errors and in order to convey the precise intention of the Court.

The proceedings before the Court are property proceedings in a de facto relationship. Proceedings were commenced on 6 September 2022. The applicant wife (Ms Tordis born in 1979, “the wife”) and the first respondent husband (Mr Manrey born in 1975, “the husband) commenced a relationship in 2001 and separated on a final basis in 2021. It was a long, troubled and difficult relationship so far as the wife was concerned.[1]
The parties have two children. One child was born in 2008. The other child was born in 2015. They both continue to live with their mother, the wife. The husband hasn't seen the children for some years (not since April 2023). The family ran a business.
The husband has been imprisoned on two occasions. Firstly, for fraud in 2015 to 2016. On that same occasion he was convicted of possession of a gun. The husband was also in prison between April 2023 and July 2024. That is post-separation and was because he had breached a domestic violence order granted in favour of the wife.
The relationship was characterised by family violence perpetrated by the husband against the wife. This was extreme at times, including vicious physical assaults. At least on one occasion the applicant wife urinated involuntarily during an assault. She has been left with long-term injuries and consequences from a health perspective including arthritis and tendinitis.

Just and equitable to make an order under section 90SM(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)

By section 90SM(2):
“(2) The court must not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.”
The parties have lived apart now for some five years. It is just and equitable that there be a property order made pursuant to section 90SM of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) and in accordance with the decision of the High Court in Stanford & Stanford (2012) 247 CLR 108.[2]

The Pool

The Court is required by section 90SM(3)(a) of the Act to identify the existing legal and equitable rights; to identify the interests in any property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them; and also, to identify the existing liabilities of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them.
Some of the pool is identifiable but there is a good deal of trouble in identifying the entirety of the pool because of the lack of cooperation by the husband. To start with, he has not appeared today. This trial has been set for quite some time. Trial directions were made in July 2025. A trial affidavit and other trial material was due by 9 February 2026. The husband failed to comply with those trial directions.
Not only that – the husband has not actually made any disclosure since 2023. Even the disclosure that he did make was limited. He has not filed an affidavit of any substance since 2022. That is some four years ago. The Court has no idea what the husband is doing at the moment. The Court does not know where he is working or what he is earning. The Court does not know what assets he holds. The Court does not know if he is running a business.
The case outline filed by the wife has been referred to and it is helpful for the Court to have regard to it. I am going make it Exhibit 2.[3]
The sale proceeds of the property at H Street, Town J (“the [H Street] property”) are now at $455,000.51. That property is joint owned. This aspect of the matter needs to be considered in some detail. The sale price was $1.2 million last year. The H Street property sold and that sale settled in April 2025.
There was previously an intervenor in these proceedings, Mr K, who was the liquidator of L Pty Ltd. That is the company through which the family ran its business. When the liquidator was appointed – as he was required to do, he sought to gather the assets of the company. He attempted to do that. He filed an affidavit on 11 August 2023 which is relied on by the wife. I note paragraph 57 of Mr K's affidavit states:
“57. Pursuant to the 22 October 2020 Order, the following vehicles (the Vehicles) were to be surrendered to me in my capacity as liquidator of the Company, or my nominee:
Vehicle Description
	
Vehicle Registration

Motor Vehicle 1
	
[...]

[...]
	
$23,000

$25,000

$unknown

Total
	
$460,500
In the above quoted paragraph – motor vehicles and other items of equipment and machinery with a total value of $460,500 are listed. Mr K points out in paragraph 59 of his affidavit of 11 August 2023 that he believes he is entitled to recover, in his capacity as liquidator of the company, the market value of the vehicles described. Mr K states that the vehicles cannot be physically recovered and, for that reason, he, as the liquidator, should be entitled to receive the sum of money included in paragraph 57 of his affidavit. The liquidator was not able to recover that sum of money.
But there was some agreement made in respect of those vehicles and the value of those vehicles. There was an agreement to settle the claim of the liquidator - a so-called deed of settlement between the husband and the liquidator - whereby the husband was to pay $160,000 to settle the entirety of the claim, including the value of those vehicles, which was worth $460,500.
The husband paid $90,000 of the $160,000 - but he paid no more.
The liquidator then continued, as he is required to, with the liquidation of the company. Mr K carried out his investigations, etc. The liquidator intervened in these property proceedings in this jurisdiction. The liquidator attempted to settle his claim for $120,000. The wife was willing to agree on that basis. The husband was not. Later, the liquidator offered to settle on the basis of a payment of $355,000. The liquidator’s claim still could not be settled.
I should note at this point that the liquidator had, at one stage, commenced proceedings in the Supreme Court and obtained various orders against the husband. These orders were not satisfied.
Then the H Street property was sold for $1.2 million and the liquidator received $502,980 to settle the claims. That included his costs, fees and outlays. The evidence discloses that the liquidator’s costs, fees and outlays were much larger than they ought to have been due to the non-cooperation of the husband.
The balance amount of $455,000 from the net sale proceeds of the H Street property at Town J now sit in trust.[4]
There is then the land at Town D which is worth $135,000. This was transferred by the husband unilaterally to a company called B Pty Ltd. B Pty Ltd are the fourth respondent in these proceedings. Mr Regenbogen is the third respondent in these proceedings. The Court is working on the basis that Mr Regenbogen controls B Pty Ltd.
Neither the third respondent nor the fourth respondent have ever entered an appearance in these proceedings. They have not filed a response document. They have not participated to any extent. This is despite the fact that they were served with the proceedings and there is an affidavit of service filed on 9 November 2023 by Mr P, the wife’s solicitor.
The third and fourth respondents were joined by order of a judicial registrar on 16 November 2023. They were also ordered to file material within a specified time. They failed to do so. This Court issued an injunction against those third and fourth respondents on 23 January 2024 preventing either of those respondents from dealing with or disposing of the Town D land. The wife does seek that that land be included in the pool.
Those respondents were joined in the proceedings. They have been served. They have been given an opportunity to be heard. Procedural fairness has been afforded to them. They have not put on any material (evidence) at any stage to explain the situation. And, in my view, the argument put forward by Counsel on behalf of the wife, Mr Bunning, is correct. That land should be included in the pool as an asset of the parties.
There is also the caravan which should be included in the pool in the amount stated, and the other figures there in the asset’s column of Exhibit 2. The total of the assets in Exhibit 2 is $682,330. The total of the liabilities $75,380. The total of the superannuation is $24,141. It should be included in the one pool with the assets – it is only a minor amount and it is convenient to do so. The total of the assets and superannuation is $706,471. When the liabilities are deducted ($75,380) the net amount totals $631,091.[5] In preparing the property pool as it appears in Exhibit 2, the lawyers for the wife have inadvertently deducted the superannuation amount from the total assets – instead of adding the superannuation amount to the assets total. I find that the pool is as follows:
Description
	
Ownership
	
Value

Assets

Sale Proceeds (H Street)

$455,051

Property 1, 2 and 3 at C Street, Town D
	
B Pty Ltd
	
$135,00

$55,000

$35,000

$1,779

Not known

Super Fund 1
	
Wife
	
$24,141

$706,471

Liabilities

Centrelink Dept
	
Wife
	
$6,509

M Financial Services Loan
	
Wife

N Pty Ltd Loan
	
Wife
	
$17,350

O Financial Services Visa
	
Wife
	
$16,654

Liabilities total
	
$75,380

Net total
	
$631,091
It is important to note here, so far as the husband is concerned, there is no proper disclosure by him to indicate what vehicles, machinery, property or other assets he might own. He has failed to make proper and ongoing disclosure as he is obligated to do. The husband has never adequately explained what happened to the vehicles listed in paragraph 22 – worth $460,500.

Contributions

As to the question of contributions, the parties were together twenty years. They both worked in the business. The wife did by far the largest amount of work in relation to the homemaking, the caring for the children and the raising of the children. There was a period from 2015 to 2016, just after the youngest child was born, when the husband through having been convicted of fraud and other offences was in prison and provided no assistance whatsoever to the raising of the children. During that time the wife continued to run the business.
In all the years that the wife worked in the business, she was never paid a separate amount (a salary) but was only given money provided to her by the husband. The husband has, in one sense, attempted to hide assets by transferring unilaterally the Town D land to B Pty Ltd. He has provided no adequate explanation as to why he did that.
The husband has failed to make proper disclosure. It has long been the case in this Court that if a party does not make proper disclosure, as required by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), on an ongoing and honest basis – the Court should not be reluctant to make orders which are in favour of the innocent party. The decision in Weir & Weir [1992] FamCA 69 is one of the many cases where this aspect of the law in this jurisdiction is stated.
I have had close regard to the difficulty of the contributions made by the wife because there was family violence. I note, in particular, section 90SM(4)(ca) –
“(4) For the purposes of subparagraph (3)(b)(i), the court is to take into account the following considerations, so far as they are relevant:

...

(ca) the effect of any family violence, to which one party to the de facto relationship has subjected or exposed the other party, on the ability of a party to the de facto relationship to make the kind of contributions referred to in paragraphs (a), (b) and (c);

...”
Family violence is one of the important matters for the Court to consider in all of the factors listed in section 90SM(4). I have taken all those factors into account even if I have not referred to them individually or separately.
Mr Bunning has submitted that, so far as contributions are concerned, the Court could assess contributions at 100% in favour of the wife given all of the matters to which I have referred. The view that I have formed is - especially taking into account the family violence which is referred to in the wife's trial affidavit; the length of time he was in prison when the second baby arrived; the fact the wife took care of the overwhelming share of the homemaking and raising of the children - I would assess contributions at 70% and 30%, in favour of the wife.

Future Circumstances

I now turn to the future circumstances. The wife is able to work. She earns $1400 a week after tax as a support worker. She still has to look after the children. She gets no financial support from the husband at all and she suffers from the ailments caused by the family violence perpetrated upon her.
Those vehicles that disappeared, the ones that were worth $460,500, they have never been recovered beyond or to the extent that $90,000 was paid to the liquidator. But so far as this family is concerned, we have there $350,000 to $400,000 worth of assets at a minimum that the Court knows about that the husband removed for his own purposes. He has not explained what happened to the assets. What we do know is, insofar as the Court is concerned, they do not exist any longer.
On the authority of Shinohara v Shinohara [2025] FedCFamC1A 126 (“Shinohara”), the Court ought not include them in the pool. The wife did not ask that they do be included in the pool. But also on the authority of Shinohara this Court must take that sort of conduct into account when one gets to section 90SM(5)(d) of the Act –
“the effect of any material wastage caused intentionally or recklessly by a party to the subject de-facto relationship, of property or financial resources of either of the parties to the subject de-facto relationship or both of them.”
Section 90SM(5)(e) needs also to be considered. This is especially so when one has regard to the fact that the liquidator's claims could have been settled for as little as $120,000 several years before they were finalised for more than $500,000. That is something that the Court should take into account.
I have had regard to all of the factors contained in section 90SM(5), including the effect of the family violence upon the wife and the stated health of the parties. We know the ages of the parties; the husband is 50 and the wife is 46. The wife does have health issues as a result of family violence perpetrated on her by the husband. But we really do not know much about the husband in terms of his state of health. The wife also has the care of the children still. That is one of the matters to be considered – by reason of section 90SM(5)(f). The wife is obligated to care for those children because she receives no child support and no financial assistance from the husband. As I say, I have had regard to all of the subsections contained in section 90SM(5).
The lack of disclosure by the husband is such that the Court does not know what other assets or resources he has, or other assets that could be included in this pool. So far as section 90SM(3)(b)(ii) is concerned and noting the considerations to which I have referred in section 90SM(5) – leads me to conclude that there should be an uplift of at least 30%. For practical purposes the wife should be entitled to 100% of the known pool.

Justice and Equity

In the Court’s view, having regard to all of the evidence, these orders are just and equitable. Certain orders have been sought that will facilitate the return of the Town D property to this property pool and thenceforth to the wife, orders under sections 106A and 106B of the Act.
It is appropriate that the money in the McKenzie Ross lawyer's trust account be paid to the trust account of Syd Murphy Proprietary Limited, trading as Creevy Horrell Lawyers Practice Trust Account. Exhibit 3 is the minute of order sought.
As to the Town D property - the sole director and shareholder of B Pty Ltd is Mr Regenbogen.[6] On 29 June 2023, the husband stated in Court before Judicial Registrar Brooks that B Pty Ltd did not pay any money for the Town D property. This is a concession made in open Court before one of the judicial officers. The husband told the Court at that time, that B Pty Ltd owns the Town D property on trust for the E Trust. The husband said that he and the children are the beneficiaries of the E Trust. A title search of the Town D property discloses that the property is owned by B Pty Ltd alone. It was not stated to be owned as a trustee of the E Trust. B Pty Ltd did not pay for the property. In this regard I note paragraphs 195 to 201 (inclusive) of the wife’s trial affidavit filed 30 January 2026.[7]
Mr Regenbogen and B Pty Ltd have been served, and as I said, they have not filed a response. They have been given every opportunity to have their say if they so wished. It seems to me to be beyond question that the Court should make the orders sought by the wife requiring a transfer, or facilitating a transfer, pursuant to section 106B and 106A of the Act. It is clearly the property of these parties to this de facto relationship. If that property truly was held on trust, the husband is required to actually provide evidence and make disclosure to prove it. He has done nothing of the sort.
In relation to the caravan which is referred to in the property pool it is listed as joint property. There does need to be some sort of order made to make it clear that it will be the wife that will be retaining that property. A declaration has been sought and that is appropriate. It is just and equitable.
This matter came before the Court in early February – 3 February 2026. The Court made an order under section 102NA of the Act – known as a banning order. The Court also made an order permitting the wife to access $11,000 for the purposes of assisting the payment of legal fees for the trial. The husband was invited by the Court to make a similar application, which would have been looked upon favourably. That was communicated to the husband in the Court that day. He did not wish to do so. I invited him to take advantage of the section 102NA order and get a Legal Aid lawyer. He did none of that. The matter came before me in February, and the Court decided that the trial would be going ahead and the parties were told as much.
On 2 March 2026 an email was sent by the husband to the solicitor for the wife formally notifying of his intention to make an application to the Court to seek an adjournment of the forthcoming proceedings. From that email he said he was going to do that the next day which would have been 3 March 2026. No such application has been filed.
Next, the husband informed the solicitor for the wife that he (the husband) was going to make another application this week for an adjournment. The husband did not do so.
The husband's lack of cooperation with the Court is evident in the following instances - failing to make proper disclosure; not filing an affidavit of any substance since 2022; not filing a trial affidavit in accordance with the trial directions; not taking advantage of the Court's invitation for him to take the benefit of some of the money in the solicitor's trust account so he could pay for a lawyer; not taking any steps that the Court knows about within any reasonable timeframe to obtain legal aid; the failure by the husband to assist the liquidator; and the husband’s obstructive attitude, which clearly led to a significantly higher amount of money having to be paid to the liquidator. It is little wonder that the Court decided to proceed today in his absence.
The matter truly has proceeded on an undefended basis. The husband is not here to defend the matter. He did not comply with the trial directions. He is required by the Rules of this Court to show up at a final hearing and I make reference to rule 15.19, rule 10.26 and rule 10.27. These are exceptional circumstances as provided for in rule 10.27(1)(h) –
“...(h) in exceptional circumstances--proceed on the non - defaulting party's evidence without hearing from the defaulting party.”
The Court is entitled to and has proceeded on the non-defaulting party's evidence, that is the wife, without hearing from the defaulting party – because he is not here. I made an order to the effect that the matter was proceeding on an undefended basis in the truest sense of the word.
The respondent would have been perfectly entitled to make an application this morning. All he had to do was to show up. But the respondent did not show up. He was required to be here at 10:00am. He was not here. He is still not here now and it is 11:30am.
The Court, at 10:54am this morning, received an email from the respondent stating:
“Attention: Family Law Court Brisbane

Attention: Justice Howard, [Mr P] and associates

I [Mr Manrey], am sending this email to confirm that due to flooding and major rain I am unable to attend court for hearing today.

(Please see attached photos)

May we seek adjournment, as filed on court portal 3rd February 2026

Sincerely

[Mr Manrey]”
I will make that Exhibit 5. I do not know where he is. He has not told the Court where he is. He has not told the Court about the extent of the flooding. He could have telephoned or contacted the Court much earlier than 10:54am. By that time, the proceedings were practically concluded. It is simply not acceptable. It is no way to litigate and there was nothing stopping him even from contacting the Court by telephone when I may have considered an application to hear him verbally with his submissions. But in the absence of an affidavit, it is very difficult and frankly not acceptable. It is not the way the Court operates.
Mr Bunning of Counsel quite rightly points out that there was no need to wait until 10:54am before sending the Court an email. As I said, the proceedings were virtually concluded by then. The Court had already made an order that the matter would proceed undefended, and without reference to any of his material and not hearing from him because he was not here.
Now there are, I am told, other outstanding applications. There is no outstanding application in a proceeding filed by the husband. I am told there may be some extant applications of an interlocutory nature that were filed by the wife that may not have been attended to or dealt with. In any event Mr Bunning submits - and I consider this is the correct approach - that all the Court needs to do is dismiss all extant applications.

Instanter application in relation to children’s passports

This morning the matter was listed for a property hearing. I am told that there is no parenting application on foot and indeed there are no parenting orders that have been made. But the children live with the mother, who is the applicant wife in the de facto property proceedings. The mother is responsible for paying for them for everything. If the father, the respondent in the property proceedings, was here he would of course have an opportunity to be heard about her application for a passport order. The mother is seeking an order that passports can be obtained without the need for the father's signature. That is the order that is really being sought here.
It seems to me that it is in the best interests of the children. It is apparent from the Reasons for Judgment that the mother has paid for everything for the children since separation five years ago. She is not receiving child support and she is not receiving any other sort of income or money from the husband to assist with the raising of the children.
It is unusual that a parenting application of this nature is made at the end of a property proceeding. But the husband has failed to attend Court. He has provided no sufficient reason. His purported reason - transmitted to the Court one hour after Court started - of being stuck somewhere amounts to little more than a further attempt to delay. In the circumstances it will be in the best interests of these children that the passports are issued and held by the mother, and that they be issued without the need for the father's signature.
I am making these orders on a final basis now as per the draft initialled by me and placed with the Court papers.

Costs

As to the question of the costs and the order that I have made at the request of the wife, I have had regard to section 114UB of the Act.
I know, in particular, that the wife made an offer to settle whereby the husband would retain the Town D land and she would get the balance. That is contained in a letter dated 29 June 2025 that was handed to the Court earlier. And he did not accept it - obviously. He did not show up for Court. And, quite apart from that, his conduct has been, quite frankly, significantly less than satisfactory. He has failed to comply with directions of the Court to file trial material. He has failed to make proper disclosure to the wife throughout the proceedings.
The husband sent an email at 10:54am saying he was not able to get here because of flooding. But, as I said earlier, that seems to me to be nothing more than an attempt at delay and it is certainly not sufficient to convince the Court to pause the proceedings or to adjourn them. The Court cannot act on late emails sent at a time when the proceedings are almost concluded. The conduct of the husband is one of the factors in section 114 to be taken into account. I am well aware of all the other factors.[8] The costs order sought is not for an additional payment of costs but only that there be a specific order about the costs. In the circumstances, the costs order sought is appropriate. It is “just”, as the section requires, and I will make it.
